input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The **Physical Twin system** under study is the **MABI MAX 100**, a **serial industrial robot** deployed in manufacturing environments. The system comprises **mechanical components** (e.g., motors, gearboxes, and encoders) and operates within a **kinematic and dynamic control domain**, requiring precise modeling of **friction, motion, and structural interactions**. The robot’s **physical state** (e.g., joint positions, velocities, and forces) is passively monitored via **one-way data acquisition** for digital shadowing purposes, emphasizing **real-time contextual data traces** (e.g., operational logs, sensor readings) aggregated for **specific manufacturing use cases** (e.g., predictive maintenance, process optimization). The system’s complexity necessitates **multi-layered abstraction** (requirements, functional/logical architecture, and technical implementation) to align with **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, as evidenced by its representation in **SysML component & connector models** (e.g., Fig. 3).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The provided documents describe a **Digital Shadow** (not a Digital Twin) as a **passive entity** with an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical system to a digital representation. It is defined as *‘a set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose’* (e.g., **friction identification** in a manufacturing or robotic system). The Digital Shadow lacks **autonomous operation** or **bidirectional data push** to the physical system, requiring **external software components** (e.g., active Digital Twin services) to interpret and process the aggregated data. No explicit **services** (e.g., monitoring, optimization, prediction, control, or visualization) are detailed for a Digital Twin in the text, as the focus is on the **Digital Shadow’s role in data collection and abstraction** for downstream analysis. **Not in Document** for Digital Twin-specific services.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The implementation of the **Digital Shadow for the MABI MAX 100** leverages the following **specific tools and enablers**:
  - **Modeling & Architecture**:
    - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: Used for **component & connector modeling** (e.g., Fig. 3) to represent the robot’s **logical architecture**, including **interfaces, interconnections, and composition** of mechanical/electrical subsystems (motors, gearboxes, encoders).
    - **UML Class Diagrams**: Employed in the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (e.g., Fig. 1) to define **concepts for data aggregation, abstraction, and traceability** in manufacturing contexts.
  - **Simulation & Execution**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink/MATLAB**: Utilized for **executable simulation models** (e.g., friction modeling in the **first axis**) to emulate **dynamic behavior** of the robot’s physical components, enabling **data-driven validation** of the digital shadow’s passive state representation.
  - **MBSE Methodologies**:
    - **Multi-Abstraction Layer Modeling**: Aligns with **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** or **SMArDT** frameworks to decompose the robot’s complexity into **requirements, functional, logical, and technical layers**, though the document does not specify the exact methodology used for the MABI MAX 100.
  - **Data Enablers**:
    - **One-Way Data Flow Protocols**: Standardized for **automated ingestion** of sensor/actuator data from the physical robot to the digital shadow, excluding **active feedback mechanisms** (e.g., PLC or control loops).
  - **Excluded Tools**: The document **does not** reference **ACME, AADL, or MontiArc** as tools used in this specific implementation; these are cited only as **general examples of Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** in the background section.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system, as described in the context of Digital Shadows, operates under a **real-time, data-driven synchronization paradigm**, where the digital representation is continuously updated via an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical system. While explicit synchronization frequencies or latency requirements are not quantified, the system inherently relies on **temporal granularity** aligned with the physical system's operational dynamics (e.g., sensor data streams). The distinction between Digital Shadows (passive, one-way) and Digital Twins (active, bidirectional) implies that the latter must support **low-latency processing** to enable real-time decision-making or feedback mechanisms, though no specific time scales (e.g., millisecond-level updates) are provided in the document. The use of **MQTT for sensor data streaming** (implied by Section IV) suggests a lightweight, event-driven protocol optimized for real-time data exchange, further reinforcing the need for **temporal alignment** between physical and digital components.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system, as modeled in the provided context, is **not explicitly described as supporting multiple instances** of Digital Twins. Instead, the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) defines a **single digital shadow instance per physical object** (e.g., a robot axis), where **FrictionShadow acts as the identification element for one digital shadow**. While the background discusses **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) that decompose systems into **abstraction layers (requirements, functions, logics, physics)**, these are **design-time concepts** and do not directly translate to runtime multiplicities of Digital Twin instances. The document does not mention **centralized vs. decentralized architectures** or **coordination mechanisms** between multiple twins, nor does it specify **scope of responsibilities** for hierarchical instances. Thus, the system is **monolithic in its shadow/twin instantiation** for the described use case.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system’s **orchestration and coordination** are grounded in **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, leveraging **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) to define **component & connector-based architectures**. The system integrates **heterogeneous modeling notations** (e.g., SysML for structure/behavior, Simulink for simulation, or C++/Java for code implementation) and **tools**, requiring **resource management** to handle cross-tool dependencies. **System-wide coordination** is achieved through **compositional hierarchies** (e.g., RFLP methodology) and **behavioral descriptions** (e.g., MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines), ensuring alignment between functional, logical, and physical layers. The **architecture pattern** is **modular and multi-perspective**, with explicit interfaces and interconnections managed via ADLs, while **active components** (e.g., simulation engines or code modules) process data autonomously, as opposed to passive digital shadows.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies to integrate heterogeneous computational models and data components across multiple abstraction layers, including **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. Key modeling approaches include **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for structural and behavioral descriptions, with support for **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc (including MontiArc Automaton for state-based behavior), and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** to define **component & connector perspectives**, including interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling is further refined using **simulation tools** like **MathWorks Simulink** or other simulation frameworks (e.g., [29], [30]), alongside general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++, Java**) for implementation. The system aggregates **contextual data traces** from physical objects, abstracting and structuring them for specific purposes (as per the Digital Shadow Reference Model [32]). Data sources are heterogeneous, requiring integration of **passive data flows** (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs) into active DT components for interpretation and processing. Model relationships are defined via **composition hierarchies** and **interface contracts**, ensuring traceability across layers. The DT constellation relies on **multi-notation modeling** to reconcile diverse stakeholder perspectives and tool-specific representations, addressing complexity through methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT** [18]–[22].
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The system incorporates **physical actuation mechanisms** driven by a **permanent magnet synchronous motor**, interfaced with a **gear transmission system** (specifically a **cycloidal gear**) to modulate torque and speed. Actuation is constrained by mechanical and control system limitations, including **safety protocols** for operational integrity. While the document does not explicitly detail **remote control interfaces**, **actuation ranges**, or **addressing mechanisms**, the presence of **gear transmission** implies precision control over motion dynamics. The system’s design suggests **closed-loop control** via feedback from sensing components (e.g., encoders) to adjust actuation parameters dynamically.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The sensing infrastructure comprises **two position encoders per drive**: a **motor encoder** mounted directly on the **permanent magnet synchronous motor** and a **secondary encoder** positioned behind the **gearbox**. These encoders provide **high-resolution positional feedback** for closed-loop control and system monitoring. While the document does not specify **sampling frequencies**, **data transmission rates**, or **accuracy/precision metrics**, the spatial distribution (motor and gearbox) ensures synchronized measurement of rotational states. Data collection is automated via **one-way data flow** to the digital representation, aligning with the **Digital Shadow Reference Model**’s passive aggregation paradigm.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin system incorporates **fidelity requirements** aligned with MBSE principles, where model accuracy and validity are ensured through **validation and verification (V&V) methods** tied to the system’s abstraction layers. Uncertainty sources are implicitly addressed via **data abstraction and aggregation** from digital shadows (as per [32]), though explicit quantification mechanisms (e.g., statistical error modeling, probabilistic validation) are not detailed in the provided context. Error detection and correction rely on **active DT components** interpreting incoming data flows, but specific protocols (e.g., real-time correction algorithms, anomaly detection frameworks) are not specified. Quality assurance processes leverage **modeling toolchains** (e.g., SysML, Simulink) and **ADLs** to enforce consistency across components, interfaces, and behaviors. The system’s fidelity is constrained by the **passive nature of digital shadows**, which lack autonomous feedback loops, necessitating external software systems to process and validate data. No explicit mention of **sampling-based validation, Monte Carlo simulations, or formal verification** (e.g., model checking) is provided.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in a Digital Shadow (DS) is characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the state of an existing physical object to its digital representation. This flow involves the **collection, aggregation, and abstraction of contextual data traces** (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs, or environmental parameters) for a specific purpose. The data is transmitted via **MQTT**, a lightweight publish-subscribe protocol optimized for IoT and industrial applications, ensuring efficient communication between physical assets and the digital model. While the document does not specify exact **transmission frequencies** or **event triggers**, the process is inherently **passive**, requiring external software components to interpret and process the incoming data. **Data quality assurance** is implied but not detailed, and no explicit **preprocessing steps** (e.g., normalization, filtering) or **data integration mechanisms** (e.g., schema validation, conflict resolution) are described beyond the aggregation and abstraction of traces. The focus is on the **unidirectional flow** without autonomous digital intervention or feedback loops to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for the Digital Shadow (DS) includes **MQTT** as the primary communication protocol for streaming sensor data from physical objects to the digital representation. The document does not specify **network topology/architecture**, **bandwidth/latency requirements**, or **reliability/redundancy mechanisms**, but the use of MQTT implies a **lightweight, event-driven** approach suitable for industrial IoT scenarios. No explicit details on **security protocols** (e.g., TLS, authentication) or **data routing mechanisms** are provided. The DS operates as a **passive entity**, relying on external systems for data interpretation and processing.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle encompasses distinct phases aligned with Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies, including **development** (where SysML, RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT methodologies are applied) and **operation** (where the Digital Twin functions as an active system). During the **development phase**, the lifecycle progresses through structured abstraction layers: **requirements**, **functional architecture**, **logical architecture**, and **product/technical layer**, utilizing architecture description languages (ADLs) such as ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs) to define components, interfaces, and interconnections. Behavioral modeling is integrated via tools like MontiArc Automaton or SysML state machines, often supplemented by simulation environments (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) or general-purpose programming languages (e.g., C++, Java). Transition management between phases involves **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **model harmonization** across diverse notations and tools, ensuring consistency in system representation. Lifecycle governance is implicitly tied to MBSE practices, emphasizing **traceability** and **validation** through iterative modeling and simulation refinements. The Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1) further contextualizes this by framing the Digital Twin as an evolution from a **passive data aggregation system** (digital shadow) to an **active, autonomous entity** capable of bidirectional data flow and real-time processing.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin (DT) development process leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, utilizing **systems engineering modeling languages** such as **SysML** for structuring system descriptions across multiple abstraction layers (e.g., requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT** are employed to manage complexity. **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** are used to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling is enhanced with tools like **MontiArc Automaton** or **SysML state machines**, often integrated with simulation tools (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) or general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++**, **Java**) for detailed behavior description. The process involves **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-notation/modeling tool coordination** to ensure comprehensive system representation. **Quality assurance** and **validation** are implicitly supported through structured modeling and simulation, though explicit testing methodologies are not detailed in the provided context. **Version control** and **evolution management** are not explicitly addressed but are inferred as critical for maintaining consistency across iterative DT development phases.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to **Industry 4.0 standards**, particularly through the implementation of **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)**, a key specification for interoperability in smart manufacturing. Data exchange and real-time communication are standardized via **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)**, a lightweight protocol for IoT and industrial telemetry. While the document does not explicitly mention broader certification frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC, IEC 62443), the use of **AAS** and **MQTT** aligns with **Industry 4.0 Reference Architecture Model (RAMI 4.0)** and **IIoT interoperability standards**. Compliance is implicitly ensured through **model-based systems engineering (MBSE)** practices, which enforce structured, traceable system representations compatible with standardized architectures.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin system incorporates **physical safety measures** through a **safety cell** restricting the robot's working range to **[-90°, 90°]** (as referenced in [44], MABI robotic arm specifications). However, the provided documents do not explicitly detail **cybersecurity protocols** (e.g., encryption for MQTT communication, despite MQTT being mentioned in [52] as a protocol for streaming data). The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS) framework** is referenced ([47], [50]) as a structural component for Digital Twin implementation, but the documents do not substantiate claims of inherent **secure data encapsulation** or **access control mechanisms**. **Risk assessment** and **fail-safe mechanisms** are not explicitly described, though **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** traceability ([43]) is used to track data point changes, which indirectly supports **safety-critical system considerations** by enabling root-cause analysis of deviations. No explicit **authentication systems** or **threat mitigation strategies** are documented.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the system as the MABI MAX 100 serial robot and correctly lists its components (motors, gearboxes, encoders) and operational domain (kinematic and dynamic control, friction modeling). It correctly notes the use of SysML component & connector models and multi-layered abstraction. However, the specific manufacturing use cases listed (e.g., 'predictive maintenance, process optimization') are not explicitly stated for this system in the text, which focuses on 'machining' and 'motion accuracy' via friction identification.

The extraction correctly distinguishes between a Digital Shadow (the focus of the paper) and a Digital Twin. It accurately describes the Digital Shadow as a passive entity with one-way data flow. It correctly notes that explicit 'Digital Twin-specific services' (active, bidirectional services like control or optimization) are not detailed in the document, as the text focuses on the shadow's role in data collection and abstraction (e.g., friction identification). 

Supporting quotes: 
- 'digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system' 
- 'Therefore, software systems require active components to interpret and process this incoming data... A Digital Twin (DT) can provide such active components' 
- 'purpose... is the understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss'

The extraction is excellent and highly specific. It correctly identifies SysML for architecture, UML for the reference model, and MathWorks Simulink for simulation. It also accurately distinguishes between the tools used in the implementation (SysML) and those mentioned only as general examples in the background (ACME, AADL, MontiArc).

block_5_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
